stories.headline: Grass fire in Thornton injures five, forces evacuations and I-25 closures
stories.summary:
A vegetation fire ignited at approximately 11:30 a.m. Wednesday at 84th Avenue and Huron Street in Thornton amid Red Flag conditions, spreading rapidly eastward along a greenbelt between residential and commercial zones. The blaze was contained at roughly 10 acres by 2:07 p.m. after a large-scale response involving firefighting resources from Thornton, Westminster, Adams County, Northglenn, Federal Heights, South Adams County and other agencies.

Five people sustained injuries related to the fire — four firefighters and one civilian — though Thornton Fire Chief Stephen Kelley described all injuries as non-critical. Law enforcement conducted door-to-door evacuations of nearby residents, a high school was fully evacuated as a precaution, and multiple roads including a stretch of Interstate 25 were closed due to smoke reducing visibility to dangerous levels.

No residential structures were lost, though some businesses along the fire's southern edge sustained damage that officials said did not appear to be structural. Crews remained on scene overnight to monitor for hotspots. The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

stories.headline: Measles case confirmed at Denver International Airport and Littleton church; free vaccinations offered
stories.summary:
A traveler with a confirmed measles case passed through Denver International Airport and attended a church service in Littleton while infectious over the weekend, Colorado public health officials announced Tuesday. The individual arrived at DIA on Saturday morning on Frontier flight 1279 from Miami and attended services and a reception at St. Mary's Catholic Church, 6853 S. Prince St. in Littleton, on Sunday.

Anyone present in the DIA gate A83 area between 9:15 a.m. and noon Saturday, at St. Mary's between 9:30 a.m. and 2 p.m. Sunday, or in the DIA gate A76 area between 9:30 p.m. Sunday and 1:30 a.m. Monday may have been exposed. The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment noted that because the passenger was fully vaccinated, transmission risk is lower but not eliminated.

State and county public health officials offered free measles vaccination clinics beginning Wednesday at the Arapahoe County Public Health Department in Englewood and at St. Mary's Church. Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, the most in a single year since at least 1996, with the majority among unvaccinated individuals.

stories.headline: Man arrested in Kansas on murder warrant in Denver Valentine's Day shooting
stories.summary:
A 20-year-old suspect was arrested Friday in Kansas in connection with a fatal shooting that occurred in Denver in the early hours of Valentine's Day. Yeanbraiker Yriarte-Valera was booked into the Wyandotte County Detention Center in Kansas City and is being held on a Denver homicide warrant.

Denver police responded to a shooting in the 1500 block of West Maple Avenue at approximately 5:15 a.m. on Feb. 14, finding a man who died at the scene and a woman who was shot in the ankle and transported to the hospital. Investigators believe the shooting stemmed from a fight at a nearby party. Yriarte-Valera faces charges including first-degree murder, four counts of attempted first-degree murder, four counts of first-degree assault and a violent crime sentence enhancer.

A second suspect, believed to have fired the shot that injured the female victim, had not been publicly identified as of Tuesday. Yriarte-Valera's first Colorado court date had not been scheduled. Anyone with information is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Two defendants plead guilty to second-degree murder in 2024 Denver Aquarium shooting
stories.summary:
Jason Trujillo, 19, and Antonio Vasquez, 21, have each pleaded guilty to second-degree murder in the Valentine's Day 2024 drive-by shooting death of 19-year-old Dacien Salazar outside the Denver Aquarium. Both are scheduled to be sentenced on March 6, according to the Denver District Attorney's Office.

Salazar and two friends had traveled from Pueblo to the aquarium on Feb. 14, 2024, when an occupant of a black sedan opened fire on them outside the venue. Investigators linked the suspects to the crime through surveillance footage, cell phone records and an anonymous tip. Trujillo and Vasquez had been arrested in May 2024 on charges of first-degree murder.

Vasquez entered his guilty plea in December 2025 and Trujillo entered his in January 2026. The case drew significant attention in part because the shooting occurred in a busy public area during a holiday, and because investigators connected it to a separate, related shooting in Pueblo.

stories.headline: Denver and Colorado business leaders respond to Supreme Court tariff ruling
stories.summary:
Hours after the U.S. Supreme Court issued a 6-3 ruling striking down Trump administration tariffs imposed under emergency powers law, Denver and Colorado business leaders said the decision provides needed clarity for economic planning. The ruling centered on tariffs levied under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, including sweeping reciprocal tariffs the administration had placed on goods from nearly every country.

J.J. Ament, president and CEO of the Denver Metro Chamber of Commerce, said the ruling "provides important clarity and helps establish the defined lanes needed for policymakers to work together toward thoughtful, consistent trade policies." Colorado Chamber of Commerce President Loren Furman said many members had experienced supply chain disruptions, increased overhead costs and uncertainty as a result of the tariffs, which affected Colorado industries ranging from wheat farming to bicycle retail to auto dealerships.

Several business leaders cautioned that relief may be temporary. Matthew Groves, president and CEO of one Colorado trade group, noted that the administration retains other legal avenues to impose tariffs. President Trump announced plans to sign an executive order imposing a 10% global tariff rate shortly after the ruling and called the court's decision incorrect.

stories.headline: Laura Clellan named permanent director of Colorado Parks and Wildlife, first woman in role since 2011 merger
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife commissioners voted unanimously Monday to confirm Laura Clellan as the agency's permanent director, a position she had held on an acting basis since Dec. 1. Clellan is the first woman to lead CPW since the division merged in 2011, according to the Colorado Sun.

Clellan brings more than three decades of military experience, including service as adjutant general of the Colorado Department of Military and Veterans Affairs and attainment of the rank of major general in the Colorado Army National Guard. She previously worked with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in Lakewood. About 150 applicants applied for the position; Clellan was selected from five finalists.

She assumes the directorship at a contentious moment for the agency. The federal government has halted Colorado's wolf reintroduction program, and hunting advocacy groups have expressed concern that the Polis administration is marginalizing their interests. Wildlife conservation groups said they have high expectations for her leadership, while some hunting organizations said they are cautiously optimistic she will manage the agency with transparency and accountability.

stories.headline: Denver Downtown Development Authority taps Urban Land Institute to chart future of Denver Pavilions
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority has engaged the Urban Land Institute to produce a recommendations report on the future of the Denver Pavilions mall and adjacent parking lots, which the DDDA purchased in December for a combined $60 million. A ULI advisory services panel is scheduled to visit Denver from April 13 to 17 and deliver its report approximately one month later.

The DDDA is paying ULI $100,000 for the panel's work. The authority has also issued two requests for proposals — one seeking retail and entertainment leasing specialists to develop a repositioning vision, and another seeking architecture and urban planning firms to reimagine the 350,000-square-foot mall and surrounding parking lots. Both RFP submissions are due March 13.

The Pavilions is approximately 60% leased and generates $5.3 million annually in rent, with parking adding another $3.2 million per year. Denver Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher said the authority hopes to transfer the property to a private-sector developer as quickly as possible rather than execute a long-term vision itself. Gart Properties, the former owner that defaulted on the property's loan, continues to manage the mall under a $379,000 contract.

stories.headline: Historic Loretto Heights administration building to become 50 rent-to-own apartments in $70M project
stories.summary:
Developer Commún plans to convert the historic Administration Building at Loretto Heights in southwest Denver into 50 rent-to-own apartments as part of a broader campus redevelopment effort, the Denver Business Journal reported. The project is valued at approximately $70 million and represents a rare application of the rent-to-own model in the Denver affordable housing market.

Under the plan, the first two floors of the building will be activated for community programming including a business incubator, while the upper floors will house the affordable residential units. A historic chapel attached to the building will be preserved and continue operating as a wedding and event venue.

The Loretto Heights campus redevelopment has been a long-running community planning effort in the Harvey Park and College View neighborhoods. The rent-to-own structure is intended to offer a pathway to homeownership for residents who might otherwise be unable to access the for-sale market.

stories.headline: Globeville affordable housing development breaks ground on $132 million mixed-use project
stories.summary:
Evergreen Real Estate Group and Globeville Redevelopment Partners have broken ground on a $132 million mixed-use development on a city-owned parcel in Denver's Globeville neighborhood, the site of a former car dealership. The project will deliver housing for families earning between 30% and 80% of the area median income.

The development represents a significant investment in one of Denver's most historically underserved and industrially impacted neighborhoods, situated along the South Platte River corridor. Globeville has faced decades of environmental and infrastructure challenges, and city officials have identified the area as a redevelopment priority.

Details on the total unit count and timeline for completion were not immediately available, but the project's scale makes it one of the larger affordable housing groundbreakings in the Denver metro in recent years.

stories.headline: Heat pump installations in Colorado more than doubled in 2025, reaching inflection point
stories.summary:
The number of heat pumps installed across five major Colorado utility service areas more than doubled in 2025 to 14,225 units, representing a twelvefold increase over three years, according to a Colorado Sun report. Industry executives and utility leaders described 2025 as an inflection point at which the technology has reached broad market acceptance.

Xcel Energy issued 10,640 heat pump rebates worth $57 million in 2025, with Denver, Boulder and Littleton among the most active markets. The Platte River Power Authority, serving Fort Collins, Longmont and surrounding communities, saw its heat pump rebates nearly quadruple to 1,256. Industry representatives attribute the acceleration to contractors becoming more comfortable installing the systems and better at stacking available rebates and incentives for customers.

The Energy Efficiency Business Coalition has a goal of heat pumps capturing 50% to 80% of the heating and cooling market by 2030. Nationally, heat pump sales in 2025 exceeded gas and oil furnace sales for the second consecutive year. Systems can range in cost from $15,000 to $30,000 depending on home size.

stories.headline: MSU Denver launches fellowship to retain and support Black male educators in Denver and Aurora schools
stories.summary:
Metropolitan State University of Denver has launched the Black Male Educator Fellowship, known as "The Den," aimed at supporting Black male teachers working in Denver Public Schools and Aurora Public Schools. The fellowship is a collaborative effort between MSU Denver's Call Me MiSTER program, the Office of Education Solutions, both school districts and the Colorado Men of Color Collaborative.

The program provides professional development, networking and community to address the isolation many Black male teachers experience. Dr. Rashad Anderson, director of the Call Me MiSTER program at MSU Denver, said Black men represent about 6% of Colorado's population but are drastically underrepresented in classrooms — 62% of Colorado school districts do not employ a single Black male teacher, according to the National Association of State Boards of Education.

Organizers say diverse teaching staffs benefit all students, and that the fellowship serves both a retention and a recruitment function. Program participants say the initiative addresses a real gap: many have never had a Black male teacher themselves and are motivated to provide that representation for the next generation of students.

stories.headline: Broncos bring back John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator ahead of 2026 season
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have hired John Morton as their offensive passing-game coordinator, reuniting him with head coach Sean Payton for a third stint together, a source confirmed to the Denver Post. Morton served in the same role for Denver in 2023 and 2024 before leaving for an offensive coordinator position with the Detroit Lions in 2025, where he was stripped of play-calling duties midseason and dismissed at year's end.

Morton had already been working in Denver's building as a consultant during the Broncos' playoff run following his departure from Detroit. He contributed directly to the team's divisional-round overtime win over the Buffalo Bills by helping install a goal-line play for offensive tackle Frank Crum that resulted in a touchdown.

The hire completes Payton's offseason staff restructuring, which began when he fired offensive coordinator Joe Lombardi two days after Denver's AFC Championship Game loss. Davis Webb was elevated to offensive coordinator and will likely handle play-calling duties, Logan Kilgore was promoted to quarterbacks coach and Ronald Curry was hired as wide-receivers coach. Morton's specific role in gameday play-calling alongside Webb remains to be determined.
